title: Implicit Bias of Projected Subgradient Method Gives Provable Robust Recovery of Subspaces of Unknown Codimension

paragraphs.text:
Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

IMPLICIT BIAS OF PROJECTED SUBGRADIENT METHOD GIVES PROVABLE ROBUST RECOVERY OF SUBSPACES OF UNKNOWN CODIMENSION
**Paris Giampouras, Benjamin D. Haeffele and René Vidal** Mathematical Institute for Data Science Johns Hopkins University Baltimore, MD, USA {parisg,bhaeffele,rvidal}@jhu.edu

## ABSTRACT

Robust subspace recovery (RSR) is the problem of learning a subspace from sample data points corrupted by outliers. Dual Principal Component Pursuit (DPCP) is a robust subspace recovery method that aims to find a basis for the orthogonal complement of the subspace by minimizing the sum of the distances of the points to the subspaces subject to orthogonality constraints on the basis. Prior work has shown that DPCP can provably recover the correct subspace in the presence of outliers as long as the true dimension of the subspace is known. In this paper, we show that if the orthogonality constraints –adopted in previous DPCP formulations– are relaxed and random initialization is used instead of spectral one, DPCP can provably recover a subspace of _unknown dimension_ . Specifically, we propose a very simple algorithm based on running multiple instances of a projected sub-gradient descent method (PSGM), with each problem instance seeking to find one vector in the null space of the subspace. We theoretically prove that under mild conditions this approach succeeds with high probability. In particular, we show that 1) all of the problem instances will converge to a vector in the nullspace of the subspace and 2) the ensemble of problem instance solutions will be sufficiently diverse to fully span the nullspace of the subspace thus also revealing its true unknown codimension. We provide empirical results that corroborate our theoretical results and showcase the remarkable implicit rank regularization behavior of the PSGM algorithm that allows us to perform RSR without knowing the subspace dimension.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Robust subspace recovery (RSR) refers to the problem of identifying an underlying linear subspace (with dimension less than the ambient data dimension) from sample data points that are potentially corrupted with outliers (i.e., points that do not lie in the linear subspace). Many methods for RSR have been proposed in the literature over the past several years (Xu et al., 2012; You et al., 2017a; Lerman & Maunu, 2018). Formulations based on convex relaxations and decompositions of the data matrix into a low-rank matrix plus a matrix of sparse corruptions – either entrywise-sparse corruptions as in Candès et al. (2011) or columnwise-sparse corruptions as in Xu et al. (2012); McCoy & Tropp (2011) – can, in certain situations, be shown to provably recover the true subspace when the dimension is unknown. However, these theoretical guarantees often require the dimension of the subspace, _d_ , to be significantly less than the ambient dimension of the data, _D_ , and these methods are not suitable for the more challenging regime of subspaces of high relative dimension (i.e., when _D[d][≈]_[1][).]

**Dual Principal Component Pursuit.** Recently, progress has been made towards solving the RSR problem in the high relative dimension regime by a formulation termed Dual Principal Component Pursuit (DPCP). As implied by its name, DPCP follows a _dual_ perspective of RSR by aiming to recover a basis for the orthogonal complement of the inliers’ subspace. As shown in (Tsakiris & Vidal, 2018), DPCP is provably robust in recovering subspaces of high relative dimension. However, a key limitation of DPCP is that it requires _a priori_ knowledge of the true subspace dimension.

DPCP was first formulated by Tsakiris & Vidal (2018) for handling subspaces of codimension _c_ = _D − d_ equal to 1 (i.e., the subspace is a hyperplane with dimension _d_ = _D −_ 1). In this case, DPCP is formulated as the optimization problem

which is nonconvex due to the spherical constraint imposed on the normal vector _**b** ∈_ S _[D][−]_[1] of the _D −_ 1 dimensional hyperplane. Tsakiris & Vidal (2018) showed that the global minimizer of (1) is a normal vector of the underlying true hyperplane when both inliers and outliers are well-distributed _or_ the ratio between the number of inliers and number of outliers is sufficiently small. Following a probabilistic point of view, (Zhu et al., 2018) presented an improved theoretical analysis of DPCP giving further insights on the remarkable robustness of DPCP in recovering the true underlying subspaces even in datasets heavily corrupted by outliers. Moreover, the authors introduced a projected subgradient method which converges to a normal vector of the true subspace at a linear rate.

**Recursive DPCP for known** _c >_ 1. Zhu et al. (2018) also proposed an extension to DPCP to subspaces with codimension _c >_ 1 via a projected subgradient algorithm that tries to learn _c_ normal vectors to the subspace in a recursive manner. Specifically, after convergence to a normal vector, the projected subgradient algorithm is initialized with a vector _orthogonal_ to the previously estimated normal vector. However, for that approach to be successful, knowledge of the true subspace codimension _c_ becomes critical. Specifically, if an underestimate of the true codimension _c_ is assumed the recovered basis for the null space, _**B**_[ˆ] , will fail to span the whole null space, _S⊥_ . On the other hand, an overestimate of _c_ will lead to columns of _**B**_[ˆ] corresponding to vectors that lie in _S_ .

**Orthogonal DPCP for known** _c_ . Zhu et al. (2019) proposed an alternative to (1) which attempts to solve for _c_ normal vectors to the subspace at once by minimizing the sum of the distances from the points to the subspace:

The authors also proposed an optimization algorithm based on the projected Riemannian subgradient method (RSGM), which builds on similar ideas as the projected subgradient method of Zhu et al. (2018) and enjoys a linear converge rate when the step size is selected based on a geometrically diminishing rule. Ding et al. (2021) provided a geometric analysis of (2) which shows the merits of DPCP in handling a) datasets highly contaminated by outliers (in the order of _M_ = _O_ ( _N_[2] )) and b) subspaces of high relative dimension. However, a key shortcoming of this approach is that, because all minimizers of (2) are orthogonal matrices, a prerequisite for recovering the correct orthogonal complement of the inliers subspace is the _a priori_ knowledge of the true codimension _c_ (see Fig. 1).

**Contributions.** In this work, we address this key limitation by proposing a framework that allows us to perform robust subspace recovery in the high relative subspace dimension regime **without** requiring _a priori_ knowledge of the true subspace dimension. In particular, our proposed approach is based on the simple idea of solving multiple, parallel instances of the DPCP formulation for subspaces of codimension one,

where _c[′]_ is assumed to be an upper bound of _c_ , i.e., _c[′] ≥ c_ . Contrary to (2), the objective function in (3) decouples over the columns _**b** i_ of matrix _**B**_ = [ _**b**_ 1 _**b**_ 2 _· · ·_ _**b** c′_ ] and thus can be solved in a parallel manner by independently applying a projected subgradient algorithm (referred to as PSGM) from _c[′]_ different _random initializations_ . Moreover, we observe that with random initialization we can get vectors sufficiently spread on the sphere that lead PSGM (initialized with those vectors) to return normal vectors of _S_ . These are all _linearly independent_ when _c[′] ≤ c_ and thus can span _S⊥_ when _c[′]_ = _c_ . If _c[′] > c_ then PSGM will return _c[′] − c_ redundant vectors that will still lie in _S⊥_ yet they will be linearly dependent (see Figure 1). That being said, we show that this simple strategy permits us to robustly recover the true subspace even without knowledge of the true codimension _c_ .

As is detailed in Sections 3 and 4, this remarkable behavior of PSGM originates from the _implicit bias_ that is induced in the optimization problem due to a) the relaxation of _orthogonality constraints_ in (3) and b) the _random initialization_ scheme that is adopted. Our specific contributions are as follows:

Second, we analyze the discrete version which is more challenging, yet of more practical interest, showing that iterates of DPCP-PSGM converge to a _scaled_ and _perturbed_ version of the initial matrix _**B**_[0] . ## 2 RELATED WORK

**Subspace Recovery.** Learning underlying low-dimensional subspace representations of data has been a central topic of interest in machine learning research. Principal Component Analysis (PCA) has been the most celebrated method of this kind and is based on the minimization of the perpendicular distances of the data points from the estimates linear subspace, Jolliffe & Cadima (2016). Albeit, it is originally formulated as nonconvex optimization problem, PCA can be easily solved in closed form using a singular value decomposition (SVD) operation (see e.g. Vidal et al. (2016)). Despite its great success, PCA is prone to failure when handling datasets that contain _outliers_ i.e., data points whose deviation from the inliers’ subspace is “large” in the _ℓ_ 2 norm sense.

**Robust Subspace Recovery (RSR).** To remedy this weakness of PCA _robust subspace recovery (RSR)_ methods attempt to identify the outliers in the dataset an recover the true underlying lowdimensional subspace of the inliers Lerman & Maunu (2018); Maunu et al. (2019). A classical approach to this problem is RANSAC Fischler & Bolles (1981), which is given a time budget and randomly chooses per iteration _d_ points and then fits a _d_ -dimensional subspace to those points and checks how the proposed subspace fits the remaining data points. RANSAC then outputs the subspace that agrees with the largest number of points. However, RANSAC’s reliance on randomly sampling points to propose subspaces can be highly inefficient when the number of outliers is high (as well as the fact that RANSAC also needs knowledge of the true subspace dimension, _d_ ). The need to tackle inherent shortcomings of RANSAC pertaining to computational complexity issues inspired alternative convex formulations of RSR, Xu et al. (2012); You et al. (2017b); Rahmani & Atia (2017); Zhang & Lerman (2014). In Xu et al. (2012) the authors decompose the data matrix as a sum of a low-rank and a column-sparse component. However, theoretical guarantees obtained for convex

formulations only hold for subspaces of relatively low-dimensional subspaces i.e., for _d ≪ D_ where _d_ and _D_ denote the subspace and the ambient dimension, respectively. To the best of our knowledge, existing RSR algorithms rely heavily on one of two key assumptions. 1) The subspace is very low-dimensional relative to the ambient dimension ( _d ≪ D_ ) or 2) _The subspace dimension is a priori known_ . Undoubtedly, the second hypothesis is rather strong in real-world applications, and many applications also do not satisfy the first assumption. Moreover, heuristic strategies for selecting the dimension of the subspace are hard to be applied in the RSR setting since they incur computationally prohibitive procedures, Lerman & Maunu (2018).

**Relation to Orthogonal Dictionary Learning (ODL).** Note that objective functions in the form of (3) show up beyond RSR problems i.e., in orthogonal dictionary learning (ODL), sparse blind deconvolution, etc., Qu et al. (2020). Specifically, based on a similar formulation the authors in Bai et al. (2019) proved that _c[′]_ = _O_ ( _c_ log _c_ ) independent random initial vectors suffice in order to recover with high probability a dictionary of size _D × c_ with high accuracy. In this paper we aim to recover a basis of the orthogonal complement of a subspace of unknown dimension instead of accurately estimating a dictionary hence our goal differs from that in Bai et al. (2019).

**Implicit bias in Robust Recovery Problems.** The notions of implicit bias and implicit regularization have been used interchangeably in the nonconvex optimization literature for describing the tendency of optimization algorithms to converge to global minima of minimal complexity with favorable generalization properties in overparameterized models, Gunasekar et al. (2018). In the context of robust recovery, the authors in You et al. (2020) showed that Robust PCA can be suitably reparametrized in such a way to favor low-rank and sparse solutions without using any explicit regularization. In this work, we use the term implicit bias for describing the convergence of DPCPPSGM to low-rank solutions, which are not necessarily global minimizers, that span the orthogonal complement of the subspace when a) orthogonality constraints in DPCP formulation are relaxed b) DPCP is overparameterized i.e., _c[′] ≥ c_ and c) PSGM randomly initialized.

## 4 DUAL PRINCIPAL COMPONENT PURSUIT IN SUBSPACES OF UNKNOWN CODIMENSION

Current theoretical results provide guarantees for recovering the true inlier subspace, when the proposed algorithms know _a priori_ of the subspace codimension _c_ , which is a rather strong requirement and is far from being true in real word applications. Here we describe our proposed approach, which consists of removing the orthogonality constraint on _**B**_ , along with a theoretical analysis that gives guarantees of recovering the true underlying subspace even when the true codimension _c_ is unknown. First we analyze a continuous version of DPCP, which arises when the number of inliers and outliers are distributed according to continuous measures and their number tends to _∞_ . The continuous DPCP incurs an optimization problem with a benign landscape that allows us to better illustrate the favorable properties of DPCP-PSGM when it comes to the convergence of its iterates. Then we extend the results to the discrete case that deals with a finite number of inliers and outliers yielding a more challenging optimization landscape.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper addresses a well-motivated and challenging problem in robust subspace recovery: removing the need for a priori knowledge of subspace codimension in DPCP-based methods. The core idea—leveraging the implicit bias of projected subgradient descent under random initialization and relaxed orthogonality constraints—is conceptually novel and aligns with recent trends in understanding implicit regularization in non-convex optimization. The empirical results are clean and support the theoretical claims, showing competitive performance against oracle-aware baselines. The work builds logically on prior DPCP formulations and provides a promising direction for extending robust recovery methods to unknown-dimensional settings.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient justification for random initialization ensuring full-rank recovery: The paper assumes that random initialization on the sphere, combined with the PSGM dynamics, will almost surely yield a set of converged vectors whose span has rank equal to the true codimension c. However, the argument in Theorem 5 and Lemma 4 rests on the projection of initial vectors onto S⊥, and there is no analysis of the probability that these projections remain linearly independent—especially when c′ is large. In high dimensions, random vectors on the sphere are nearly orthogonal, but their projections onto a fixed c-dimensional subspace become increasingly aligned as D grows, risking rank collapse. The paper does not quantify this risk or provide a lower bound on the smallest singular value of the projected initialization matrix. Without such a bound, the claim that random initialization suffices to span S⊥ is speculative, particularly in the finite-sample regime.

- Overly optimistic assumption on discrete-to-continuous approximation in Theorem 7: Theorem 7 relies on the error term \hat{\bm{\Delta}} being small when the number of outliers and inliers is large, but the bound in condition (18) includes terms like (\eta_O + c_{O,max} - c_d) that do not necessarily vanish at a rate sufficient to ensure full-rank recovery unless M and N are extremely large. The analysis assumes that discrete averages converge quickly to their continuous counterparts, but in practice—especially with structured or clustered outliers—this convergence can be slow. The paper does not provide finite-sample concentration bounds for \|e_O^{i,k}\| or \|e_X^{i,k}\|, nor does it discuss how the required sample complexity scales with D and c. This weakens the practical relevance of the theoretical guarantee, as real datasets may not meet the implicit sample size requirements.

- Lack of robustness analysis under structured or adversarial outliers: The theoretical analysis assumes outliers are either uniformly distributed on the sphere or well-distributed in the ambient space, which ensures c_{O,max} ≈ c_D and \eta_O ≈ 0. However, the paper does not consider structured outliers—such as those lying near a different low-dimensional subspace—which are common in real applications and could drastically alter the optimization landscape. For instance, if outliers are themselves clustered, the subgradient direction may align with their structure, leading PSGM to converge to vectors orthogonal to the outlier subspace rather than the inlier one. The empirical results use uniformly sampled outliers, so they do not test robustness in more realistic, adversarial, or clustered settings. This limits the generality of the claims about 'robust recovery.'

- Ambiguity in the definition and role of 'implicit bias': The paper attributes the rank-revealing behavior of PSGM to 'implicit bias,' but this term is used more as a label than a mechanistic explanation. While Lemma 4 shows that the limit vectors are determined by the projection of initial vectors onto S⊥, the actual bias toward low-rank solutions is not analyzed in terms of optimization dynamics or landscape geometry. For example, there is no discussion of whether the DPCP objective has spurious minima in S or whether the subgradient flow naturally avoids them. The comparison to implicit regularization in overparameterized models is suggestive but not rigorously connected. A more precise characterization—such as showing that PSGM avoids directions in S due to larger subgradient norms—would strengthen the theoretical contribution.

- Missing comparison with baseline methods that estimate dimension: The paper compares DPCP-PSGM only to DPCP variants (RSGM, IRLS) that require known codimension, showing that they fail when c is overestimated. However, it omits comparison with existing RSR methods that can estimate subspace dimension from data, such as L1-PCA with scree plots, robust covariance estimation with eigenvalue thresholding, or outlier pursuit with rank selection. These methods are standard in practice and would provide a more meaningful benchmark for the 'unknown codimension' claim. Without such comparisons, it is unclear whether the proposed method offers advantages beyond the specific DPCP framework or simply shifts the burden of dimension estimation to the choice of c′.

- No simulation demonstrating failure under structured outliers: The paper claims robust recovery but only tests outliers drawn uniformly from the sphere. A key structural gap is the absence of experiments with structured outliers, such as those lying in a different low-dimensional subspace, which are common in practice and could cause the subgradient direction to align with outlier geometry rather than the inlier nullspace. Readers will wonder whether the method fails silently in such settings. To address this, the authors should run a simulation where 30% of outliers lie in a 10-dimensional subspace and compare recovery accuracy against the uniform case. This would demonstrate whether the theoretical assumption of well-distributed outliers is practically consequential.

- Missing finite-sample concentration bounds for key error terms: The analysis in Theorem 7 relies on the error term $\hat{\bm{\Delta}}$ being small, but the paper does not provide finite-sample concentration bounds for $\|e_O^{i,k}\|$ or $\|e_X^{i,k}\|$ as functions of $N$, $M$, $D$, and $c$. Without these, the practical applicability of the probabilistic guarantee in (18) is unclear. For instance, it is unknown how large $N$ must be for the bound to hold when $D=100$ and $c=5$. The authors should derive or cite concentration inequalities for the deviation of discrete averages from their continuous counterparts under sub-Gaussian or bounded assumptions, and include a figure showing how the required $N$ scales with $D$.

- No comparison to dimension-estimating baselines: The paper positions its method against DPCP variants that require known codimension but omits comparison to standard RSR approaches that estimate dimension from data, such as L1-PCA with eigenvalue thresholding or outlier pursuit with rank selection. This omission undermines the claim of solving the 'unknown codimension' problem in a general sense. A natural question is whether DPCP-PSGM outperforms these methods or merely offers an alternative within the DPCP framework. The authors should compare recovery accuracy and estimated dimension on synthetic data using L1-PCA and a robust covariance estimator, reporting both subspace distance and rank estimation error across outlier ratios.

- No computation of rank recovery threshold in parametric model: The theory predicts that the solution matrix rank reveals the true codimension $c$, but the paper does not compute this explicitly for a fully specified model. For example, it should work through the case where inliers are uniform on $\mathcal{S} \cap \mathbb{S}^{D-1}$ and outliers uniform on $\mathbb{S}^{D-1}$, and derive the minimum outlier ratio $M/N$ for which $\mathrm{rank}(\bar{\bm{B}}^*) = c$ with high probability. This would ground the intuition and allow direct comparison with DPCP's known recovery thresholds. Without such a worked example, the novelty of the rank-revealing mechanism remains abstract.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a finite-sample probabilistic lower bound on the smallest singular value of the projected initialization matrix \mathcal{P}_{S_\perp}(B^0) to justify full-rank recovery under random initialization.

- Derive concentration bounds for the error terms e_O^{i,k} and e_X^{i,k} and clarify the sample complexity required for Theorem 7 to hold in practical regimes.

- Include experiments with structured or clustered outliers to demonstrate robustness beyond the uniform distribution assumption.

- Sharpen the analysis of 'implicit bias' by connecting it to the geometry of the DPCP objective or subgradient dynamics, rather than treating it as a post-hoc label.

- Add comparisons with standard RSR methods that estimate subspace dimension automatically, such as robust PCA with rank selection or L1-PCA with cross-validation.

comments.title: Incorrect expression for spherical mean in cd
comments.quote:
Moreover, $c_{\bm{X},\min}$ and $c_{\bm{X},\max}$ converge to the same value as $N\to\infty$ provided the inliers are uniformly distributed in the subspace i.e., $c_{\bm{X},\min}\to c_{d},c_{\bm{X},\max}\to c_{d}$, where $c_{d}$ is given as the average height of the unit hemisphere on $\mathbb{R}^{d}$,

\[ c_{d}:=\frac{(d-2)!!}{(d-1)!!}\begin{cases}\frac{2}{\pi},\;\text{if}\;\;d\;\;\text{is even},\\
\;\;1,\;\text{if}\;d\;\;\text{is odd}\end{cases}\;\;\;\text{where}\;k!!=\begin{cases}k(k-2)(k-4)\cdots 4\cdot 2,\;k\;\;\text{is even},\\
k(k-2)(k-4)\cdots 3\cdot 1,\;k\;\;\text{is odd}\end{cases} \] (6)
comments.explanation: The expression for $c_d$ does not match the expected absolute value of a coordinate on $\mathbb{S}^{d-1}$. For $d=3$, the formula yields $1/2$, but direct integration gives $\int_{\mathbb{S}^2} |x_1| d\sigma = \pi/4 \approx 0.785$. The correct expression is $c_d = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi(d-1)}} \frac{\Gamma(d/2)}{\Gamma((d-1)/2)}$, which arises from the distribution of projections of uniform spherical vectors. The current double factorial form corresponds to Gaussian projections, not uniform spherical ones, and invalidates the convergence claims in (6) unless corrected.

comments.title: Invalid projection-normalization commutation in continuous analysis
comments.quote:
By projecting the PSGM iterates given in (10) onto $\mathcal{S}_{\perp}$ we have,

$\mathcal{P}_{\mathcal{S}_{\perp}}(\bm{b}_{i}^{k+1})=(1-\mu_{i}^{k}pc_{D})\mathcal{P}_{\mathcal{S}_{\perp}}(\hat{\bm{b}}_{i}^{k})\text{ and }\ \mathcal{P}_{\mathcal{S}_{\perp}}(\hat{\bm{b}}_{i}^{k+1})=\mathcal{P}_{\mathcal{S}_{\perp}}(\mathcal{P}_{\mathbb{S}^{D-1}}(\bm{b}_{i}^{k+1}))$ (11)
comments.explanation: The derivation assumes that projecting onto $\mathcal{S}_\perp$ commutes with normalizing onto the sphere, but normalization is nonlinear and distorts relative component sizes. While $\mathcal{P}_{\mathcal{S}_\perp}(\bm{b}_i^{k+1})$ evolves linearly, $\mathcal{P}_{\mathcal{S}_\perp}(\hat{\bm{b}}_i^{k+1})$ depends on the full direction of $\bm{b}_i^{k+1}$, not just its $\mathcal{S}_\perp$ component. If $\bm{b}_i^{k+1}$ has a large inlier component, normalization disproportionately shrinks the $\mathcal{S}_\perp$ part, breaking the scaling argument used in Lemma 4. This invalidates the claimed recurrence and undermines the continuous-case analysis.

comments.title: Unjustified convergence of error series in discrete analysis
comments.quote: we denote $\hat{\bm{b}}^{*}_{i}=\mathcal{P}_{\mathbb{S}^{D-1}}\left(\mathcal{P}_{\mathcal{S}_{\perp}}(\bm{b}^{0}_{i})-\mathcal{P}_{\mathcal{S}_{\perp}}(\hat{\bm{\delta}}_{i})\right)$, where $\hat{\bm{\delta}}_{i}=\lim_{K\to\infty}\bm{\delta}^{K}_{i}$.
comments.explanation: The existence of $\hat{\bm{\delta}}_i = \lim_{K\to\infty} \bm{\delta}_i^K$ assumes convergence of a weighted sum of subgradient errors, but the weights $\prod_{j=0}^k \|\bm{b}_i^j\|_2 / (1 - \mu_i^j M c_D)$ depend on prior iterates and may grow unbounded. Without bounds on $\|\bm{b}_i^k\|_2$ away from zero and control over step-size decay, the series may diverge. For instance, if $\mu_i^k = \Theta(1/k)$, the product term can grow exponentially, overwhelming summability. The analysis provides no justification for convergence under the stated assumptions.

comments.title: Incorrect singular value condition for full-rank recovery
comments.quote: If $\sigma_{c^{\prime}}(\bm{B}^{0})>\|\hat{\bm{\Delta}}\|_{2}$ then matrix $\bm{A}=\bm{B}^{0}-\hat{\bm{\Delta}}$ is full-rank.
comments.explanation: The condition $\sigma_{c'}(\mathbf{B}^0) > \|\hat{\bm{\Delta}}\|_2$ does not guarantee full column rank of $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{B}^0 - \hat{\bm{\Delta}}$. Matrix perturbation theory requires $\sigma_{\min}(\mathbf{B}^0) > \|\hat{\bm{\Delta}}\|_2$ to ensure $\sigma_{\min}(\mathbf{A}) > 0$. The notation $\sigma_{c'}(\mathbf{B}^0)$ refers to the $c'$-th singular value, which equals $\sigma_{\min}(\mathbf{B}^0)$ only if $\mathbf{B}^0$ has full column rank. But $\sigma_{c'}(\mathbf{B}^0)$ could be zero if $c' > \mathrm{rank}(\mathbf{B}^0)$, making the condition vacuous. The lemma must reference the smallest singular value explicitly.

comments.title: Unverified equivalence of discrete and continuous subgradient terms
comments.quote: Note that the average outliers and inliers terms are discrete versions of the corresponding continuous average terms  $c_{D}\pmb{b}_{i}$  and  $c_{d}\hat{\mathbf{s}}_{i}$  where  $\hat{\mathbf{s}}_i = \mathcal{P}_S(\pmb {b}_i)$ , respectively, Tsakiris & Vidal (2018).
comments.explanation: The claim that $\mathbf{x}_{\mathbf{b}_i} \to c_d \mathcal{P}_S(\mathbf{b}_i)$ assumes symmetric inlier distribution on $\mathcal{S} \cap \mathbb{S}^{d-1}$, but this is not stated. If inliers concentrate in a half-subspace, $\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{Sgn}(\mathbf{b}_i^\top \mathbf{x})\mathbf{x}]$ may not align with $\mathcal{P}_S(\mathbf{b}_i)$. Similarly, $\mathbf{o}_{\mathbf{b}_i} \to c_D \mathbf{b}_i$ requires spherical symmetry of outliers, which is not assumed. The constants $c_d, c_D$ are undefined in terms of their dependence on $d$ and $D$, leaving the approximation unjustified.

comments.title: Ambiguous and potentially ill-defined constants in Theorem 7
comments.quote:
If

$1-C_{1}\sqrt{\frac{c^{\prime}}{D}}-\frac{\epsilon}{\sqrt{D}}>\sqrt{c^{\prime}}\kappa(\bm{\eta_{O}}+c_{\bm{O},\max}-c_{d})$ (18)

where $\kappa=\max_{i}\frac{M\mu_{i}^{0}}{\beta^{K_{0}/K_{t}}(1-r_{i})}$ and $r_{i}=\frac{\left(1+\mu_{i}^{0}(N(\bm{\eta_{X}}+c_{\bm{X},\max})+M(\bm{\eta_{O}}+c_{\bm{O},\max}))\right)}{1-\mu_{i}^{0}Mc_{D}}\beta^{1/K_{*}}$
comments.explanation: Condition (18) contains multiple ambiguities: $\bm{\eta_O}, c_{\bm{O},\max}$ are written in bold but used as scalars; $K_t$ appears in $\beta^{K_0/K_t}$ but is undefined (likely a typo); and $\beta^{1/K_*}$ with $\beta < 1$ implies $\beta^{1/K_*} \to 1$, so $r_i$ may exceed 1, making $\kappa$ undefined. The expression for $r_i$ lacks derivation from the step-size rule (8), and without explicit definitions of $\beta, K_0, K_*$, the condition cannot be verified or implemented. These notational issues undermine the practical applicability of the theorem.

comments.title: Missing bound on unprojected iterate norms
comments.explanation: The recursive expression (16) depends on $\|\mathbf{b}_i^k\|_2$, but no lower bound is provided. If $\|\mathbf{b}_i^k\|_2 \to 0$, the product $\prod_{k=0}^{K-1} \frac{1 - \mu_i^k M c_D}{\|\mathbf{b}_i^k\|_2}$ may diverge, invalidating the convergence analysis. While $\|\hat{\mathbf{b}}_i^k\|_2 = 1$, the pre-projection norm $\|\mathbf{b}_i^k\|_2$ can be small if the subgradient update is large. The analysis requires $\|\mathbf{b}_i^k\|_2 \geq \rho > 0$ uniformly in $k$, but this is not established, risking numerical instability and theoretical gaps.

comments.title: Gap in argument for spanning $\mathcal{S}_\perp$
comments.quote: In order to guarantee that $\mathrm{span}(\bm{B}^{*})\equiv\mathcal{S}_{\perp}$ it thus suffices to ensure that $\mathrm{rank}\left(\bm{B}^{*}\right)=c$. Here we show that a sufficient condition for this to hold is that the matrix $\bm{A}=\bm{B}^{0}-\hat{\bm{\Delta}}$ is full-rank.
comments.explanation: The argument conflates full column rank of $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{B}^0 - \hat{\bm{\Delta}}$ with $\mathrm{rank}(\mathbf{B}^*) = c$. If $c' > c$, $\mathbf{B}^*$ cannot have rank $c$ and full column rank simultaneously. The correct requirement is that $\mathcal{P}_{\mathcal{S}_\perp}(\mathbf{B}^0 - \hat{\bm{\Delta}})$ has rank $c$. Even if $\mathbf{A}$ is full-rank, its projection onto $\mathcal{S}_\perp$ may have reduced rank. The paper must analyze the rank of the projected matrix, not the original, to support the spanning claim.

comments.title: Incorrect convergence claim for unprojected iterates
comments.quote: then the iterates $\bm{b}^{k}$ converge to a normal vector of $\mathcal{S}$.
comments.explanation: The sequence $\bm{b}^k$ consists of unnormalized vectors from the subgradient step and may diverge in norm depending on step sizes. Convergence to a direction in $\mathcal{S}_\perp$ can only be established for the projected iterates $\hat{\bm{b}}^k \in \mathbb{S}^{D-1}$. For example, if $\mu^k$ decays too slowly, $\|\bm{b}^k\|_2 \to \infty$, so $\bm{b}^k$ cannot converge. The cited work (Zhu et al., 2018) analyzes $\hat{\bm{b}}^k$, not $\bm{b}^k$. The conclusion must refer to the normalized sequence.

comments.title: Unjustified uniform convergence of outlier statistics
comments.quote: Further, if the outliers are uniformly distributed on the sphere, then $c_{\bm{O},\max}\to c_{D}$ and $c_{\bm{O},\min}\to c_{D}$ where $c_{D}$ is defined as in (6), *(Zhu et al., 2018)*.
comments.explanation: Pointwise convergence $\frac{1}{M}\|\bm{O}^\top \bm{b}\|_1 \to c_D$ for fixed $\bm{b}$ does not imply uniform convergence over $\bm{b} \in \mathbb{S}^{D-1}$. For finite $M$, there may exist directions nearly orthogonal to all outliers, making $\|\bm{O}^\top \bm{b}\|_1$ small. Uniform convergence requires $M \gg D$ due to covering number arguments, but this condition is not stated. Without it, $c_{\bm{O},\min}, c_{\bm{O},\max}$ may not converge to $c_D$, undermining the analysis of outlier influence.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes DPCP-PSGM, a method for robust subspace recovery that eliminates the need to know the true subspace codimension by leveraging implicit bias from relaxed orthogonality constraints and random initialization. The work makes theoretical contributions for both continuous and discrete settings, with empirical validation on synthetic data.

The paper addresses an important limitation in robust subspace recovery by removing the requirement of known codimension. The theoretical analysis is comprehensive, covering both continuous and discrete cases, and the empirical results demonstrate the method's effectiveness. The connection between implicit bias and robust recovery is novel and well-motivated.

Overview issues:

- Tightness of Codimension Overestimation Analysis: Theorem 7 provides a sufficient condition for successful recovery when overestimating codimension, but the condition (18) involves problem-dependent constants that may be loose. The paper does not characterize how much overestimation can be tolerated in practice, nor does it provide guidance on choosing c'. This limits the method's practical applicability since users need to select c' without clear bounds on acceptable overestimation. The analysis should include tighter bounds or practical guidelines for c' selection based on problem parameters.

- Missing Sample Complexity Analysis: While Theorem 7 provides probabilistic guarantees, the paper lacks explicit sample complexity bounds relating the required number of inliers N and outliers M to subspace dimensions D, d, and c. Such bounds are standard in robust subspace recovery literature and crucial for understanding data requirements. Without these bounds, practitioners cannot determine whether their dataset size is sufficient for successful recovery. The analysis should include explicit sample complexity requirements as functions of the problem parameters.

- Limited Validation on Real-World Data: The empirical evaluation only includes synthetic data experiments and lacks validation on real-world datasets where the assumptions of uniform distribution may not hold. Real data often exhibits approximate subspace structure and non-uniform outlier distributions, which could challenge the method's robustness. The paper should include experiments on standard real-world benchmarks for robust subspace recovery to demonstrate practical performance beyond idealized settings.

- Computational Complexity and Scalability Concerns: The paper does not provide detailed computational complexity analysis or convergence rate guarantees for the discrete DPCP-PSGM algorithm. While the method is presented as simple, its practical efficiency compared to spectral methods like RSGM remains unclear. The analysis should include iteration complexity, wall-clock time comparisons, and discussion of scalability to high-dimensional problems, particularly since the method requires running multiple parallel instances.

- Assumption Limitations for Practical Applications: The theoretical analysis assumes inliers lie exactly in a d-dimensional subspace and outliers are well-distributed. These assumptions may be violated in practice where data exhibits approximate subspace structure or clustered outliers. The paper does not analyze robustness to such model misspecification, which limits understanding of the method's performance on real data. The analysis should include sensitivity analysis to violations of these assumptions.

- Missing worked parametric example: The paper presents theoretical results for both continuous and discrete versions of DPCP-PSGM but never works through a concrete, fully-specified example. For instance, the paper could compute the solution for a simple case where D=3, d=1 (so c=2), with specific inlier and outlier distributions. This would allow readers to verify the implicit bias mechanism explicitly and see how the random initialization leads to the correct orthogonal complement. Such a worked example is standard in optimization theory papers to build intuition about the algorithm's behavior.

- No demonstration of failure cases: The paper claims robustness to overestimated codimension but never shows a concrete example where the method fails when the assumptions are violated. Specifically, the paper should demonstrate a data generating process where the initialization angle condition (Assumption 1) is violated, or where the outliers are not well-distributed, leading to recovery failure. This would establish the necessity of the theoretical conditions and help practitioners understand when the method might break down.

- Unspecified implementation details: While Algorithm 1 provides the basic PSGM update steps, critical implementation details are missing. The paper does not specify how to choose the step-size parameters (μ⁰, β, K₀, K_*) in practice, nor does it provide guidance on determining when the algorithm has converged. For a method claiming practical relevance, these details are essential for reproducibility and for others to implement the algorithm effectively.

- Missing comparison to recursive DPCP baseline: The paper compares against DPCP-IRLS and RSGM but does not include the recursive DPCP method from Zhu et al. (2018) as a baseline. Since recursive DPCP also handles codimension > 1 (albeit requiring knowledge of c), a comparison would show how much is gained by removing the codimension requirement. The paper should include experiments comparing recursive DPCP with known c against DPCP-PSGM with overestimated c' to quantify the trade-off between requiring dimension knowledge and robustness to overestimation.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add explicit sample complexity bounds as functions of D, d, c, N, and M to characterize data requirements

- Include experiments on real-world robust subspace recovery benchmarks to validate practical performance

- Provide tighter analysis of codimension overestimation tolerance with practical guidelines for selecting c'

- Add computational complexity analysis and empirical runtime comparisons with baseline methods

- Analyze robustness to model misspecification when assumptions about exact subspace structure or uniform outlier distribution are violated

comments.title: Abstract overstates theoretical contribution
comments.quote: We theoretically prove that under mild conditions this approach succeeds with high probability. In particular, we show that 1) all of the problem instances will converge to a vector in the nullspace of the subspace and 2) the ensemble of problem instance solutions will be sufficiently diverse to fully span the nullspace of the subspace thus also revealing its true unknown codimension.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims the method works under "mild conditions" but the paper's actual analysis requires specific geometric conditions (Assumption 1: principal angles bounded by arctan(Nc_X,min/(Nη_X + Mη_O))) and matrix conditioning requirements (Lemma 6: lower bound on smallest singular value of B^0). These are not mild conditions - they require careful initialization and specific relationships between inlier/outlier distributions. The abstract should accurately reflect that success depends on these specific technical conditions rather than vague "mild conditions."

comments.title: Missing orthogonality constraint clarification
comments.quote: Dual Principal Component Pursuit (DPCP) is a robust subspace recovery method that aims to find a basis for the orthogonal complement of the subspace by minimizing the sum of the distances of the points to the subspaces subject to orthogonality constraints on the basis. Prior work has shown that DPCP can provably recover the correct subspace in the presence of outliers as long as the true dimension of the subspace is known. In this paper, we show that if the orthogonality constraints –adopted in previous DPCP formulations– are relaxed and random initialization is used instead of spectral one, DPCP can provably recover a subspace of *unknown dimension*.
comments.explanation: The abstract states that orthogonality constraints are "relaxed" but doesn't specify how. The paper's method (DPCP-PSGM) actually removes explicit orthogonality constraints entirely and relies on the implicit bias from random initialization to achieve orthogonal solutions. This is a significant methodological difference, not just a relaxation. The abstract should clarify that orthogonality constraints are completely removed, not relaxed, and that orthogonal solutions emerge as an emergent property of the algorithm.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in DPCP formulation
comments.quote:
We re-write the DPCP formulation given in (1) as

$\min_{\bm{B}\in\mathbb{R}^{D\times c^{\prime}}}\|\tilde{\bm{X}}^{\top}\bm{b}\|_{1}=\|\bm{X}^{\top}\bm{b}\|_{1}+\|\bm{O}^{\top}\bm{b}\|_{1}\;\;\;\mathrm{s.t.}\;\;\;\;\|\bm{b}\|_{2}=1$ (4)
comments.explanation: The minimization variable is inconsistent: the objective uses vector $\bm{b}$ but the min operator specifies matrix $\bm{B}$. The equality $\|\tilde{\bm{X}}^{\top}\bm{b}\|_{1}=\|\bm{X}^{\top}\bm{b}\|_{1}+\|\bm{O}^{\top}\bm{b}\|_{1}$ suggests $\tilde{\bm{X}}=[\bm{X}\;\bm{O}]$ is a concatenation, but this is not defined. Rewrite equation (4) as $\min_{\bm{b}\in\mathbb{R}^{D}}\|\bm{X}^{\top}\bm{b}\|_{1}+\|\bm{O}^{\top}\bm{b}\|_{1}\;\;\;\mathrm{s.t.}\;\;\;\;\|\bm{b}\|_{2}=1$ because the algorithm in (5) operates on a single vector $\bm{b}$.

comments.title: Dimensional inconsistency in geometric quantity definition
comments.quote: defined as $c_{\bm{X},\min}=\frac{1}{N}\min_{\bm{b}\in\mathbb{S}^{d-1}\cap\mathcal{S}}\|\bm{X}^{\top}\bm{b}\|_{1}$ and $c_{\bm{X},\max}=\frac{1}{N}\max_{\bm{b}\in\mathbb{S}^{D-1}\cap\mathcal{S}}\|\bm{X}^{\top}\bm{b}\|_{1}$
comments.explanation: Since $\mathcal{S}$ is a $d$-dimensional subspace of $\mathbb{R}^D$, the intersection $\mathbb{S}^{D-1}\cap\mathcal{S}$ equals $\mathbb{S}^{d-1}$ (the unit sphere within the subspace). Both definitions should use the same domain for consistency. Rewrite as $c_{\bm{X},\min}=\frac{1}{N}\min_{\bm{b}\in\mathbb{S}^{d-1}}\|\bm{X}^{\top}\bm{b}\|_{1}$ and $c_{\bm{X},\max}=\frac{1}{N}\max_{\bm{b}\in\mathbb{S}^{d-1}}\|\bm{X}^{\top}\bm{b}\|_{1}$ where $\mathbb{S}^{d-1}$ denotes the unit sphere within subspace $\mathcal{S}$.

comments.title: Missing sphere projection in PSGM update
comments.quote: $\bm{b}_{i}^{k+1}=\hat{\bm{b}}_{i}^{k}-\mu_{i}^{k}(pc_{D}\hat{\bm{b}}_{i}^{k}+(1-p)c_{d}\hat{\mathbf{s}}_{i}^{k})\text{ and }\ \hat{\bm{b}}_{i}^{k+1}=\mathcal{P}_{\mathcal{S}_{\perp}}(\bm{b}_{i}^{k+1})\;,i=1,2,\ldots,c^{\prime}$ (10)
comments.explanation: Equation (10) is inconsistent with the unit norm constraint in Lemma 2. The projection $\mathcal{P}_{\mathcal{S}_{\perp}}$ maps to the orthogonal complement but doesn't preserve unit norm. Equation (11) shows the correct two-step projection process. Rewrite equation (10) as $\bm{b}_{i}^{k+1}=\hat{\bm{b}}_{i}^{k}-\mu_{i}^{k}(pc_{D}\hat{\bm{b}}_{i}^{k}+(1-p)c_{d}\hat{\mathbf{s}}_{i}^{k})\text{ and }\ \hat{\bm{b}}_{i}^{k+1}=\mathcal{P}_{\mathbb{S}^{D-1}}(\mathcal{P}_{\mathcal{S}_{\perp}}(\bm{b}_{i}^{k+1}))\;,i=1,2,\ldots,c^{\prime}$ to satisfy both constraints.

comments.title: Undefined term $\hat{\mathbf{s}}_{i}^{k}$ in update rule
comments.quote: $\bm{b}_{i}^{k+1}=\hat{\bm{b}}_{i}^{k}-\mu_{i}^{k}(pc_{D}\hat{\bm{b}}_{i}^{k}+(1-p)c_{d}\hat{\mathbf{s}}_{i}^{k})\text{ and }\ \hat{\bm{b}}_{i}^{k+1}=\mathcal{P}_{\mathcal{S}_{\perp}}(\bm{b}_{i}^{k+1})\;,i=1,2,\ldots,c^{\prime}$ (10)
comments.explanation: The term $\hat{\mathbf{s}}_{i}^{k}$ appears without definition. Based on Lemma 2's objective function involving $\cos(\phi_i)$ where $\phi_i$ is the angle from subspace $\mathcal{S}$, this should be the projection onto the inlier subspace. Add the definition $\hat{\mathbf{s}}_{i}^{k} = \mathcal{P}_{\mathcal{S}}(\hat{\bm{b}}_{i}^{k})$ after equation (10) to make the update rule mathematically complete.

comments.title: Projection error term justification missing
comments.quote:
. Following a similar path as in the continuous case we next project the iterates of (13) onto  $S_{\perp}$ ,

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathcal {P} _ {\mathcal {S} _ {\perp}} \left(\boldsymbol {b} _ {i} ^ {k + 1}\right) = \mathcal {P} _ {\mathcal {S} _ {\perp}} \left(\hat {\boldsymbol {b}} _ {i} ^ {k}\right) - \mu_ {i} ^ {k} \left(M \mathcal {P} _ {\mathcal {S} _ {\perp}} \left(c _ {D} \hat {\boldsymbol {b}} _ {i} ^ {k} + \boldsymbol {e} _ {\boldsymbol {O}} ^ {i, k}\right) + N \underbrace {\mathcal {P} _ {\mathcal {S} _ {\perp}} \left(c _ {D} \hat {\boldsymbol {s}} _ {i} ^ {k} + \boldsymbol {e} _ {\boldsymbol {X}} ^ {i , k}\right)} ^ {0}\right) \tag {14} \\ = (1 - \mu_ {i} ^ {k} M c _ {D}) \mathcal {P} _ {\mathcal {S} _ {\perp}} (\hat {\boldsymbol {b}} _ {i} ^ {k}) - \mu_ {i} ^ {k} M \mathcal {P} _ {\mathcal {S} _ {\perp}} (\boldsymbol {e} _ {\
comments.explanation: The error term $e_X^{i,k} = x_{\hat{b}_i^k} - c_D \hat{s}_i^k$ is not guaranteed to lie in subspace $S$. Since $x_{\hat{b}_i^k} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{j=1}^{N} Sgn(\hat{b}_i^{k\top} x_j) x_j$ is a weighted average of inlier vectors, it lies in $S$. However, $\hat{s}_i^k = \mathcal{P}_S(\hat{b}_i^k)$ is the projection of $\hat{b}_i^k$ onto $S$, so $c_D \hat{s}_i^k$ also lies in $S$. Therefore $e_X^{i,k}$ lies in $S$, making its projection onto $S_\perp$ indeed zero. Add the following justification after equation (14): "Note that $x_{\hat{b}_i^k}$ is a convex combination of inlier vectors $\{x_j\}_{j=1}^N \subset S$, thus $x_{\hat{b}_i^k} \in S$. Since $\hat{s}_i^k = \mathcal{P}_S(\hat{b}_i^k) \in S$, we have $e_X^{i,k} = x_{\hat{b}_i^k} - c_D \hat{s}_i^k \in S$, which implies $\mathcal{P}_{S_\perp}(e_X^{i,k}) = 0$."

comments.title: Missing justification for Lemma 6 condition
comments.quote: If $\sigma_{c^{\prime}}(\bm{B}^{0})>\|\hat{\bm{\Delta}}\|_{2}$ then matrix $\bm{A}=\bm{B}^{0}-\hat{\bm{\Delta}}$ is full-rank.
comments.explanation: Lemma 6 claims that if the smallest singular value of $B^0$ exceeds the spectral norm of $\hat{\Delta}$, then $A = B^0 - \hat{\Delta}$ is full-rank. This follows from Weyl's inequality for singular values: $\sigma_{\min}(A) \geq \sigma_{\min}(B^0) - \|\hat{\Delta}\|_2$. However, the paper provides no justification for this claim, which is crucial for the convergence argument. Add the following justification after Lemma 6: "By Weyl's inequality for singular values, we have $\sigma_{\min}(A) = \sigma_{\min}(B^0 - \hat{\Delta}) \geq \sigma_{\min}(B^0) - \|\hat{\Delta}\|_2 = \sigma_{c'}(B^0) - \|\hat{\Delta}\|_2 > 0$, which implies $A$ is full-rank."

comments.title: Undefined step-size parameters in Theorem 7
comments.quote: where $\kappa=\max_{i}\frac{M\mu_{i}^{0}}{\beta^{K_{0}/K_{t}}(1-r_{i})}$ and $r_{i}=\frac{\left(1+\mu_{i}^{0}(N(\bm{\eta_{X}}+c_{\bm{X},\max})+M(\bm{\eta_{O}}+c_{\bm{O},\max}))\right)}{1-\mu_{i}^{0}Mc_{D}}\beta^{1/K_{*}}$ then with probability at least $1-2\exp(-\epsilon^{2}C_{2})$ (where $C_{1},C_{2}$ are absolute constants), Algorithm 1 with a piecewise geometrically diminishing step size rule will converge to a matrix $\hat{\bm{B}}^{*}$ such that $\mathrm{span}(\hat{\bm{B}}^{*})\equiv\mathcal{S}_{\perp}$.
comments.explanation: The parameters $\beta, K_*, K_0, K_t$ are referenced but not defined in this section. The parenthetical reference to equation (8) is insufficient since equation (8) is not provided in this section. Without these definitions, readers cannot verify the convergence condition. Add the following definitions after the statement of Theorem 7: "Here $\beta \in (0,1)$ is the decay factor, $K_0$ is the number of initial iterations with constant step size, $K_t$ is the total number of iterations, and $K_* = K_t - K_0$ is the number of iterations with geometric decay. These parameters are defined in the piecewise geometric step-size rule $\mu_i^k = \mu_i^0$ for $k < K_0$ and $\mu_i^k = \mu_i^0 \beta^{k/K_*}$ for $k \geq K_0$."

comments.title: Figure reference inconsistency
comments.quote: On the contrary, DPCP-PSGM displays a remarkably robust behavior (Fig. 2(middle)) even without knowing the true value of  $c$ , performing similarly to RSGM when the latter knows beforehand the correct codimension (Fig. 2(left)).
comments.explanation: The text references Fig. 2(middle) for DPCP-PSGM results and Fig. 2(left) for RSGM with true c, but the caption states the left panel shows DPCP-PSGM with c'=10, the middle panel shows RSGM with true c, and the right panel shows RSGM with c'=10. This creates a direct contradiction between the figure description and the textual reference. The text should be corrected to match the figure caption: DPCP-PSGM results are in Fig. 2(left), RSGM with true c is in Fig. 2(middle), and RSGM with overestimated c is in Fig. 2(right).

comments.title: Unsubstantiated 100% recovery claim
comments.quote: It can be observed that DPCP-PSGM achieves  $100\%$  for all different codimensions for  $r = 0.6$ .
comments.explanation: The claim of 100% success rate for all codimensions at outlier ratio r=0.6 appears to be an overstatement based on the visual evidence in Fig. 3. The figure shows 10 trials per codimension value, and while most points appear to hit the true values, there are visible deviations (particularly at c=16 and c=18) where some trials show estimated values different from the true codimension. The authors should either provide the exact numerical results to support the 100% claim or qualify it to reflect the actual success rate observed in the experiments.

comments.title: Inconsistent terminology for nullspace/orthogonal complement
comments.quote: We theoretically prove that under mild conditions this approach succeeds with high probability. In particular, we show that 1) all of the problem instances will converge to a vector in the nullspace of the subspace and 2) the ensemble of problem instance solutions will be sufficiently diverse to fully span the nullspace of the subspace thus also revealing its true unknown codimension.
comments.explanation: The abstract uses "nullspace of the subspace" but earlier it refers to "orthogonal complement of the subspace." In linear algebra, these are distinct concepts: the nullspace refers to the kernel of a linear operator, while the orthogonal complement refers to all vectors orthogonal to a subspace. For robust subspace recovery, the goal is to find the orthogonal complement S_⊥, not a nullspace. This terminology inconsistency should be corrected to use "orthogonal complement" throughout for mathematical precision.

comments.title: Figure 1 caption inconsistency
comments.quote: Note that in (left)  $\mathrm{rank}(\bar{B}^*)$  (where  $\bar{B}^* = [b_1^*, b_2^*, b_3^*]$ ) equals to the true codimension  $c = 2$  of  $S$  and  $\mathrm{span}(B^*) \equiv S_\perp$  while in (right)  $B^*$  is orthogonal hence  $\mathrm{rank}(B^*) = 3$  with  $b_2^* \in S$ .
comments.explanation: There's an inconsistency in the notation used in the figure caption. The left side refers to $\bar{B}^*$ and $\mathrm{span}(B^*)$ while the right side refers to $B^*$. Based on the context, it appears that $\bar{B}^*$ should be used consistently throughout the caption to represent the recovered matrix from the proposed DPCP-PSGM approach. The mixed notation creates confusion about whether $\bar{B}^*$ and $B^*$ refer to the same or different matrices. Rewrite as: "Note that in (left) $\mathrm{rank}(\bar{B}^*)$ (where $\bar{B}^* = [b_1^*, b_2^*, b_3^*]$ ) equals the true codimension $c = 2$ of $S$ and $\mathrm{span}(\bar{B}^*) \equiv S_\perp$ while in (right) $\bar{B}^*$ is orthogonal hence $\mathrm{rank}(\bar{B}^*) = 3$ with $b_2^* \in S$ ."

comments.title: Undefined constants $c_D$ and $c_d$ in Lemma 2
